normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.requirement_title: The app uses the user interface securely.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-PLATFORM-3 (Description): Sensitive data has to be displayed in the UI in many situations (e.g. passwords, credit card details, OTP codes in notifications). This control ensures that this data doesn't end up being unintentionally leaked due to platform mechanisms such as auto-generated screenshots or accidentally disclosed via e.g. shoulder surfing or sharing the device with another person.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must handle sensitive UI content (passwords, credit card details, OTP codes, account balances, private messages) without leaking through platform mechanisms — auto-generated app-switcher screenshots, notification previews on the lock screen, accessibility-service readouts, screen recording, or copy-to-clipboard exposure. Apps must protect sensitive screens from capture, prevent overlay/tapjacking attacks, and refuse to render system-mimicry prompts that could trick users into supplying credentials.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies to every Tier 2+ app that displays sensitive content in its UI. Per Constitution Pillar 5 (Secure Architecture & Runtime), UI integrity discipline activates at Tier 2+ where the app handles content distribution and analytics; security_manifest.ui_integrity is the per-app declaration block (present at Tier 2+ per the existing schema). The blueprint schema does not declare a discrete sensitive-UI-content boolean because virtually every Tier 2+ app displays content the user would not want exposed in app-switcher previews or shoulder-surfed (private posts, account balances, partial card numbers, notification content). The tier-floor applicability is the honest proxy until the schema gains a precise sensitive-UI-surface field. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 2+ displays sensitive content because account state, private messages, and personalized content are baseline Tier 2+ surfaces.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 5 covers MASVS-PLATFORM-3 via two layered traces: SEC-PILLAR-5-UI-INTEGRITY (primary) and SEC-PILLAR-5-SCREEN-PROTECTION (supporting). UI-INTEGRITY is the primary Constitutional control for UI-side discipline — tapjacking prevention via view-isolation (iOS) and filterTouchesWhenObscured (Android), prevention of system-prompt mimicry that could trick users into supplying credentials, and overlay-detection where applicable. SCREEN-PROTECTION (supporting) covers the screen-capture vector — FLAG_SECURE on sensitive Android screens, capture-mask views on iOS that swap to a placeholder during applicationWillResignActive (the moment iOS takes the app-switcher screenshot). The mapping is partial because PLATFORM-3 spans multiple vectors — tapjacking/overlay (UI-INTEGRITY) and screen capture (SCREEN-PROTECTION) — and neither single trace covers both. Shoulder-surfing protection (private text fields with secureTextEntry / inputType=textPassword) and accessibility-service exposure (importantForAccessibility="noHideDescendants" on sensitive content) require auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The two-trace coverage substantively addresses the tapjacking and screen-capture halves; broader UI-side leakage (notification content, clipboard, accessibility) is auditor-evaluation surface.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: applicationWillResignActive / sceneWillResignActive handler swaps sensitive view content to a neutral placeholder (logo, brand background) before iOS captures the app-switcher screenshot
iOS: sensitive UITextField uses isSecureTextEntry = true; sensitive UILabel content cleared on backgrounded transitions for the duration of the inactive window

Android: sensitive Activities call getWindow().setFlags(LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE, LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE) in onCreate — prevents screenshots and screen recording, blanks the surface in the app-switcher overview
Android: sensitive Views call setFilterTouchesWhenObscured(true) — refuses touch events when another window obscures the view (tapjacking defense)
Android: sensitive content uses android:importantForAccessibility="noHideDescendants" where accessibility-service readout would leak (e.g., one-time codes, card numbers)

Notification content rendering respects platform-secure modes: iOS shows notification preview content only when device is unlocked (UNNotificationContent default); Android uses NotificationCompat.VISIBILITY_SECRET for sensitive notifications
security_manifest.ui_integrity declares: list of sensitive surfaces requiring screen-capture protection, surfaces requiring tapjacking prevention, surfaces requiring system-mimicry refusal
failure_indicators: iOS: sensitive UI content visible in the app-switcher screenshot because no applicationWillResignActive view-swap is in place — credentials, card numbers, private messages exposed to anyone with physical device access
iOS: password / OTP input field without isSecureTextEntry = true — content visible to over-the-shoulder observation and accessibility-service readout
Android: sensitive Activity without FLAG_SECURE — screenshot leak into app-switcher preview AND user-initiated screenshots (Power+Volume) capture sensitive content
Android: sensitive button without setFilterTouchesWhenObscured(true) — accept-button taps can be coerced by overlay attacks (tapjacking) showing a fake innocuous UI on top
Notification content that displays full OTP codes, full card numbers, or full private message bodies on the lock screen — credential / private-content leak to anyone glancing at the device

Sensitive content copy-to-clipboard without auto-clear on backgrounded transition — clipboard contents persist across apps (notably accessible by paste targets including keyboard apps with clipboard access)
System-mimicry prompts: app rendering a fake iOS / Android system-style dialog (e.g., "iCloud password required") to harvest credentials — explicit violation of platform UX integrity
Sensitive content rendered via UILabel / TextView without secure-text disposition AND read aloud by accessibility services because importantForAccessibility wasn't restricted
false_positive_traps: UI handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test mocks legitimately display fixture credentials; these are not production violations.

Non-sensitive UI surfaces (marketing landing screens, public help content, public-facing posts) do NOT require FLAG_SECURE / view-swap — the violation surface is sensitive content without protection, not all content with protection. security_manifest.ui_integrity declares which surfaces are sensitive.

Notification previews with redacted content ("You have a new message" without preview text) on the lock screen are NOT violations — the violation surface is FULL sensitive content shown in the preview, not the existence of notifications.

Use of accessibility services for legitimate purposes (custom accessibility actions, VoiceOver labels for non-sensitive UI) is NOT a violation — the violation surface is sensitive content readable via accessibility without importantForAccessibility restriction.

Clipboard usage for non-sensitive copy (e.g., URL share for non-private content) is NOT a violation — the violation surface is sensitive content copied without auto-clear policy declared in security_manifest.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 5 UI-integrity discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-UI-INTEGRITY) as primary evidence toward MASVS-PLATFORM-3 coverage on the tapjacking and system-mimicry vectors
uses Constitution Pillar 5 screen-protection discipline (SEC-PILLAR-5-SCREEN-PROTECTION) as supporting evidence toward MASVS-PLATFORM-3 coverage on the screen-capture vector
declares sensitive UI surfaces in security_manifest.ui_integrity (screen-capture protection, tapjacking prevention, system-mimicry refusal) per MASVS-PLATFORM-3 requirement
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-PLATFORM-3. MASVS-PLATFORM-3 is mapped to two Constitution v0.5 traces in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-5-UI-INTEGRITY (primary, tapjacking + system-mimicry layer) and SEC-PILLAR-5-SCREEN-PROTECTION (supporting, screen-capture layer). Neither trace alone covers PLATFORM-3's full breadth — UI-INTEGRITY handles overlay attacks and platform-UX integrity, SCREEN-PROTECTION handles the screen-capture vector — the partial-mapping label is honest about this. Claims about sensitive-UI protection are valid only for the surfaces declared in security_manifest.ui_integrity; surfaces not declared as sensitive carry no protection claim. Claims about shoulder-surfing resistance are valid only when sensitive text fields use platform secure-text disposition (isSecureTextEntry on iOS, inputType=textPassword on Android). Claims about accessibility-service leak prevention require explicit importantForAccessibility restriction on sensitive Views — Constitution does not require this declaration in security_manifest, so the claim chain runs through auditor evaluation rather than manifest declaration. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
